topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The postcard, a flimsy piece of glossy paper depicting a sun-drenched coastline I’d never seen, lay on my cluttered desk. My fingers traced the faded blue ocean, the movement slow, heavy. Sarah. Moving to Seattle. It had been a quiet realization, a slow seep of unwelcome news into the otherwise dull rhythm of my Tuesday afternoon. I stared at the framed photo of us from that disastrous camping trip last summer, the forced laughter in the picture feeling brittle now. I didn't move from my chair. My gaze drifted to the window, where the afternoon light seemed thick and slow, refusing to penetrate the gloom inside. A low hum of emptiness settled in my chest, a physical weight that made the simple act of lifting my coffee mug feel like scaling a mountain. I picked up my phone, intending to text her, but the screen remained stubbornly dark.

[story 2]

"So, you're actually leaving?" The words felt like gravel in my throat. I managed a weak nod, the movement stiff. Across the room, Mark was animated, recounting some trivial anecdote about his new apartment. I listened, nodding at the right intervals, my attention snagging on the vibrant energy radiating off him, a warmth I couldn't seem to summon. The news had landed like a sudden drop in temperature. My limbs felt heavy, weighted down by an invisible cloak. I kept picking at a loose thread on the worn fabric of my sweater, my eyes fixed on the pattern, unable to focus on the conversation. Every word Mark spoke seemed to bounce off an internal wall, leaving me stranded in a quiet, muted space, observing the world move on without me.

[story 3]

I reread the email three times. The sheer distance—three thousand miles separating us—felt monumental, an impossible gulf. My hands were cold, clammy against the smooth surface of the laptop. I hadn't changed my position in the armchair since the notification popped up. The room seemed to shrink around me, the colors leaching out of the wallpaper. I could feel a profound inertia, a refusal in my muscles to engage with anything beyond the immediate vicinity of my body. When Sarah called later, her voice bright and full of plans, I could only offer shallow responses. "That sounds... great," I managed, the sound thin and distant, like a radio signal struggling to reach a receiver. I just wanted the quiet to swallow me whole, to stop the constant, dull thrumming inside my head.
